title: Introducing a new rabbit to resident rabbits
subtitle: Why rabbit bonding requires more than putting two rabbits in the same pen, and how to do it safely.
excerpt: Rabbits are territorial and will fight severely when a stranger is placed directly into their established space. Successful introductions require a structured process of neutral-territory introductions, gradual side-by-side housing, and supervised contact before the pair or group shares permanent accommodation. Skipping any stage risks injury.
sourceNotes: RSPCA: rabbit bonding guidelines. Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund: introducing rabbits. House Rabbit Society: bonding and social needs.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of gradually introducing two or more rabbits until they cohabit peacefully, groom each other, and rest in contact. Bonding can take days to weeks depending on the personalities of the rabbits involved and the method used. A bonded pair or group is more secure and shows fewer stress behaviours than solitary-housed rabbits.

Attack behaviour in rabbits directed at unfamiliar rabbits or humans entering an established rabbit's core territory. Includes lunging, biting, circling, and fur-pulling. Most severe when an unknown rabbit is placed directly into the resident's existing pen. Reduced or absent in neutral territory where neither rabbit has a territorial advantage.

content.text: The most common mistake in rabbit introductions is placing a new rabbit directly into the resident's housing.
in an established space is immediate and can be severe. Rabbits chasing briefly may already have bite wounds by the time you step in.

A new rabbit should be housed completely separately for at least two weeks before any contact with resident rabbits. This allows time for signs of respiratory infection (sneezing, nasal discharge) or other illness to become apparent before the new rabbit has any nose-to-nose contact. Check for mites at the start of the quarantine period.

Choose a space neither rabbit has used before: a garden area not normally used by the resident rabbit, or a clean pen neither has been in. Place both rabbits in this space at the same time and observe from a close distance. Some chasing and mounting (by either sex, regardless of reproductive status) is normal dominance behaviour, not aggression. Separate them if prolonged chase with biting occurs, or if either rabbit is pinned and cannot escape. Return them to their separate housing. Repeat daily or twice daily.

Once the rabbits are showing less aggression in neutral territory (moving past each other, grooming themselves near each other), move them into adjacent pens separated by a secure double-mesh barrier. They can see and smell each other continuously. Swap their bedding and feeding bowls so each gets accustomed to the other's scent. Feed them on opposite sides of the shared barrier so they associate the presence of the other rabbit with a positive experience.

When the rabbits show mutual grooming or resting in contact through the barrier, they are ready for a supervised first shared session in a large neutral space. A successful session involves lying near each other, mutual grooming, and normal individual behaviour. Once you have seen this, move them into a thoroughly cleaned pen that has been scrubbed to remove the resident rabbit's scent markers.
is confirmed when the rabbits groom each other and sleep in contact without chasing.
